Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: Potentially adverse events are analyzed to better understand associated activities
Implementation Example: Ex1: Use security information and event management (SIEM) or other tools to continuously monitor log events for known malicious and suspicious activity
Implementation Description: Alma Security uses AWS CloudTrail and GuardDuty for SIEM-like log monitoring. Detection rules are tuned based on threat intelligence with current TTD at 9 hours. The Incident Response Enhancement project addresses 24/7 monitoring gaps. Log analysis covers malicious activity patterns across AWS accounts with expanded use cases added quarterly.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review CloudTrail and GuardDuty configuration
2. Examine detection rule tuning
3. Verify TTD metrics
4. Assess 24/7 monitoring gaps
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: AWS-native detection stack (CloudTrail, GuardDuty, VPC Flow Logs, DNS query logs) monitors log events for malicious activity; no third-party SIEM. TTD has improved from the 81-hour Oct 2024 baseline to 9 hours, but the Jan 2027 target is under 4 minutes and there is no 24/7 analysis coverage. The Incident Response Enhancement project ($150K) funds after-hours monitoring and expanded use cases.
Q2 Examine: GuardDuty findings console, detection use-case inventory, TTD trend report
Q2 Test: Replayed three benign-anomaly scenarios; two generated alerts within SLA
Q3 Observations: Detection reached the passing bar this quarter: weekly ROC-cadence rule tuning is running, quarterly use-case expansion delivered the S3 and SSH lateral-movement detections, and self-measured TTD improved from 9 hours to roughly 6 hours. Coverage is consistent across all AWS accounts with exceptions tracked. After-hours analysis still depends on PagerDuty paging of on-call staff; the 24/7 rota from the IR Enhancement project is staffed for Q4 go-live.
Q3 Examine: Detection rule change log; TTD weekly metric series; use-case backlog
Q3 Test: Re-ran three GuardDuty findings end-to-end to measure detection-to-triage time
Q4 Observations: Sustained at the passing bar with measurement discipline: the 24/7 alerting rota went live in December, TTD self-measures at roughly 4 hours against the January 2027 sub-four-minute commitment, and rule tuning held its weekly cadence through the quarter. Score holds at 5 rather than rising because full monitoring coverage and the minutes-grade target remain unmet — the remaining gap is capacity and automation, funded into 2027 (risk R3).
Q4 Examine: Rota schedule and paging records; December TTD metric series
Q4 Test: Verified after-hours alert acknowledged within rota SLA on two sampled nights
Action Plan: Complete IR Enhancement project: 24/7 alert coverage via PagerDuty rotation, quarterly use-case expansion, TTD measured weekly against the <4-minute Jan 2027 target (risk R3).

Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: Information is correlated from multiple sources
Implementation Example: Ex2: Use event correlation technology (e.g., SIEM) to collect information captured by multiple sources
Implementation Description: Event correlation uses: (1) CloudTrail aggregating API events across accounts, (2) GuardDuty correlating findings from multiple AWS sources, (3) custom scripts for cross-source analysis. Multi-account GuardDuty findings are centralized. Script updates planned to enhance correlation capabilities across additional sources.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review CloudTrail aggregation
2. Examine GuardDuty correlation
3. Verify custom correlation scripts
4. Assess multi-account centralization
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: Event correlation is limited to what GuardDuty natively joins across CloudTrail, VPC Flow, and DNS sources. There is no correlation layer spanning AWS, SentinelOne endpoint telemetry, Palo Alto logs, and the on-prem ALMA domain, so cross-source attack paths — the INC-2024-02 lateral-movement pattern — still require manual assembly by an analyst.
Q2 Examine: Detection architecture diagram, source-coverage matrix
Q2 Test: Traced a simulated multi-source event; correlation required manual pivoting across three consoles
Q3 Observations: No score movement: cross-source correlation spanning AWS telemetry, SentinelOne, Palo Alto logs, and the on-prem ALMA domain still does not exist, and the INC-2024-02 lateral-movement pattern would still require manual assembly by an analyst. The Q3 evaluation of correlation options within the AWS-native stack completed and recommended building the top ten use cases incrementally; funding was approved late in the quarter, so implementation had not started at fieldwork close.
Q3 Examine: Correlation options evaluation memo; approved use-case list
Q4 Observations: Held at 3 — the correlation build slipped behind the 24/7 rota and encryption work in the team's capacity queue (risk R1 in practice). Two of ten use cases are drafted, none in production. The honest read: correlation is the largest remaining DETECT gap for exactly the attack class we already experienced, and it carries into the FY2027 detection engineering plan with a March 2027 delivery commitment.
Q4 Examine: Use-case build status; detection engineering FY2027 plan
Action Plan: Carry forward to FY2027: implement the ten approved cross-source correlation use cases starting with the INC-2024-02 SSH lateral-movement pattern; first two in production by 2027-03-31 (risk R3).

Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: The estimated impact and scope of adverse events are understood
Implementation Example: Ex1: Use SIEMs or other tools to estimate impact and scope, and review and refine the estimates
Implementation Description: Impact and scope estimation happens ad hoc during triage using GuardDuty severity and asset context from ServiceNow, but there is no documented estimation method and no refinement loop after incidents close. Post-incident reviews of the two 2024 incidents produced scope timelines retroactively, showing the capability exists in people, not process..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Impact and scope estimation happens ad hoc during triage using GuardDuty severity and asset context from ServiceNow, but there is no documented estimation method and no refinement loop after incidents close. Post-incident reviews of the two 2024 incidents produced scope timelines retroactively, showing the capability exists in people, not process.
Q2 Examine: Triage runbook, post-incident review reports for INC-2024-01 and INC-2024-02
Q3 Observations: The impact and scope estimation step shipped into the triage runbook on schedule: analysts now record an initial blast-radius estimate with asset-criticality lookup from ServiceNow at declaration time. Execution is real but young — the September retro found estimates recorded on four of five qualifying events, with one missed during a busy on-call night. The monthly estimate-versus-actual review has run twice.
Q3 Examine: Triage runbook v3; five most recent event records
Q3 Test: Walked one live GuardDuty event through the new estimation step
Q4 Observations: Consistent execution across the full quarter: every qualifying event carried a scope estimate, and the monthly retro loop is refining the estimation heuristics — December's review tightened the biometric-data-path multiplier after a November event under-scoped. The capability has moved from people to process, meeting the minimum bar with tracked exceptions and a working improvement loop.
Q4 Examine: Q4 event records; retro minutes with heuristic changes
Action Plan: Add an impact/scope estimation step with asset-criticality lookup to the triage runbook; review estimates against actuals in monthly D&R retro (risk R3).

Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: Information on adverse events is provided to authorized staff and tools
Implementation Example: Ex1: Use cybersecurity software to generate alerts and provide them to the security operations center (SOC), incident responders, and incident response tools
Implementation Description: GuardDuty generates alerts routed to the security team via Slack with severity-based prioritization. SOC procedures define handoff between business hours staff and on-call rotation. Alert triage follows the priority matrix with escalation paths. Progress toward 24/7 coverage planned through STS1 hiring strategy.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review alert routing configuration
2. Examine SOC procedures
3. Verify on-call rotation
4. Assess alert triage effectiveness
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: GuardDuty and CloudWatch alerts route to the D&R team via PagerDuty for after-hours paging, and Slack for business hours. Routing works but is single-threaded through the 15-person security team (risk R1) — no tiered escalation, and alert context does not auto-enrich with asset owner or criticality, so responders lose minutes on lookup.
Q2 Examine: PagerDuty routing rules, Slack alert channel history sample
Q2 Test: Fired a synthetic GuardDuty finding; page delivered in under 2 minutes with bare context
Q3 Observations: Alert enrichment and escalation landed ahead of the October due date and pushed this control past target: alerts now auto-enrich with ServiceNow asset owner and criticality before paging, and the two-tier escalation path is documented and drill-tested. Time-lost-to-lookup, measured before and after, dropped materially, and the enrichment coverage metric is reviewed in the weekly D&R meeting — the control is now measured, not just performed.
Q3 Examine: Enrichment pipeline config; escalation drill records; coverage metric dashboard
Q3 Test: Triggered a synthetic alert and verified enriched context and tier-two escalation
Q4 Observations: Sustained above target through the quarter with the 24/7 rota now consuming the enriched alerts: enrichment coverage held at 98 percent or better each month, and the December escalation drill met its timing thresholds. No regression observed; remaining single-threading risk is a staffing question (risk R1) rather than an alert-handling design gap, and it is tracked there rather than here.
Q4 Examine: Monthly coverage metrics; December drill record
Action Plan: Auto-enrich alerts with ServiceNow asset context; define a two-tier escalation path as part of the Incident Response Enhancement project ($150K).

Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: Cyber threat intelligence and other contextual information are integrated into the analysis
Implementation Example: Ex1: Securely provide cyber threat intelligence feeds to detection technologies, processes, and personnel
Implementation Description: Threat intelligence consumption is limited to the feeds GuardDuty bundles natively. There is no curated CTI capability aligned to Alma's threat profile — extortion crews targeting biometric data, auth-bypass fraud actors, and supply-chain attackers — so detection engineering is not prioritized by who actually targets a continuous-authentication SaaS..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Threat intelligence consumption is limited to the feeds GuardDuty bundles natively. There is no curated CTI capability aligned to Alma's threat profile — extortion crews targeting biometric data, auth-bypass fraud actors, and supply-chain attackers — so detection engineering is not prioritized by who actually targets a continuous-authentication SaaS.
Q2 Examine: GuardDuty threat list configuration, detection backlog
Q3 Observations: First real movement on threat intelligence: the five-actor threat profile (extortion crews, product fraud actors, supply-chain attackers, nation-state collection, insider risk) is now documented and mapped to detection coverage, exposing that most existing rules serve only the first actor class. The lightweight CTI intake — curated feeds triaged weekly against the actor map — began operating in September; it has not yet driven a detection-engineering cycle.
Q3 Examine: Threat-actor map; CTI intake log, first three weeks
Q4 Observations: The CTI loop closed its first full cycle: October intake produced the Q4 detection-engineering priorities, which shipped two fraud-actor detections on the product authentication path — the first coverage aimed at threat actor two. The process runs weekly with documented triage, but a single quarter of operation leaves reliability unproven; one more consistent cycle earns the passing score. Prioritization now genuinely reflects who targets a continuous-authentication SaaS.
Q4 Examine: Q4 priority memo; shipped detection rules; weekly triage log
Action Plan: Sustain the weekly CTI cadence through Q1 2027 and ship the supply-chain actor detections (risk R6 linkage) to close the remaining gap to target.

Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: Incidents are declared when adverse events meet the defined incident criteria
Implementation Example: Ex1: Apply incident criteria to known and assumed characteristics of activity in order to determine whether an incident should be declared
Implementation Description: Incident declaration follows documented criteria defining thresholds by severity level. The SOC Manager has authority to declare incidents when adverse events meet criteria. The process includes: (1) applying criteria to event characteristics, (2) documenting declaration rationale in tickets, (3) lessons learned feeding continuous improvement.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review incident declaration criteria
2. Examine sample declarations
3. Verify SOC Manager authority
4. Assess lessons learned integration
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: Incident declaration criteria were rebuilt after the 2024 incidents and are now the strongest link in the DETECT chain: documented thresholds by event type, a named declaration authority (D&R Lead, CISO backup), and declaration drill-tested quarterly. Both tabletop exercises this year declared within criteria, and the metric (time-to-declaration) is tracked and reviewed monthly.
Q2 Examine: Incident declaration criteria doc, Q1 and Q2 tabletop reports
Q2 Test: Walked through declaration decision tree against the INC-2024-02 fact pattern; correct outcome
Q3 Observations: Incident declaration criteria remain the strongest DETECT control and held above target: the Q3 tabletop declared within criteria, time-to-declaration stayed within threshold on both real events this quarter, and the metric review continued monthly. The criteria were extended with a data-exposure declaration path informed by the INC-2024-01 pattern, keeping the control aligned with the threat profile as detection coverage expands upstream.
Q3 Examine: Declaration criteria v4; tabletop record; monthly metric reviews
Q3 Test: Tabletop exercise included declaration-timing verification
Q4 Observations: Sustained above target: the December ransomware tabletop — run jointly with the recovery exercise — declared within criteria, and all real declarations this quarter met the timing threshold with the 24/7 rota now holding declaration authority after hours under the documented backup chain. Twelve consecutive months at or above target with measurement make this the reference control for the DETECT function.
Q4 Examine: Q4 declarations; joint tabletop record; rota authority documentation

Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: Networks and network services are monitored to find potentially adverse events
Implementation Example: Ex1: Monitor DNS, BGP, and other network services for adverse events
Implementation Description: Network monitoring is performed through: (1) CloudTrail for API activity, (2) VPC Flow Logs for network traffic, (3) DNS query logging. The selected ASM vendor will provide external perimeter monitoring. SOC dashboards display network service status with alerting for anomalies. Coverage expands per the implementation plan.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review network monitoring tools
2. Verify CloudTrail and VPC Flow Logs
3. Assess ASM vendor selection progress
4. Examine SOC dashboards
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: Internal network monitoring is solid on AWS (VPC Flow Logs, DNS query logs) but the external perimeter is still scanned only bimonthly with no continuous discovery — risk R2, the register's most exposed gap for an internet-facing authentication product. The ASM implementation ($100K) is mid-deployment; the original 2026-06 target has slipped to 2026-09.
Q2 Examine: VPC Flow Log coverage report, last two bimonthly external scan reports, ASM project plan
Q2 Test: Verified an intentionally exposed test port surfaced in the last scan cycle — 41 days after exposure
Q3 Observations: The ASM deployment completed its discovery phase in September — external scanning now runs continuously and found eleven previously unknown internet-facing assets in its first month, validating the risk R2 premise. The score holds at 4 because triage discipline is not yet real: the 48-hour triage SLA for new exposures was met for only about half of findings, and ownership assignment depends on the asset inventory work that has slipped (risk R4, revised to March 2027).
Q3 Examine: ASM discovery reports; triage SLA records, first month
Q3 Test: Verified a newly exposed test service appeared in ASM within 24 hours
Q4 Observations: Held at 4: continuous external discovery is running and the perimeter is no longer dark between scans, but the control cannot pass while triage of what discovery finds remains unreliable — December SLA compliance was 62 percent, constrained by unclear asset ownership pending the inventory rebuild (risk R4). The gap has moved from visibility to accountability; the fix is the inventory completion, not more scanning.
Q4 Examine: Q4 ASM findings and SLA compliance report
Action Plan: Tie exposure triage to the rebuilt asset inventory (risk R4, revised 2027-03): every ASM finding auto-assigns an owner from inventory data; SLA compliance target 90 percent by 2027-06 (risk R2).

Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: The physical environment is monitored to find potentially adverse events
Implementation Example: Ex1: Monitor logs from physical access control systems (e.g., badge readers) to find unusual access patterns (e.g., deviations from the norm) and failed access attempts
Implementation Description: Badge-reader logs exist for the Redwood City HQ and the server room housing the ALMA domain controller, but nobody reviews them on a cadence — they are pulled only when an investigation asks. Failed-access alerting is not configured.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Badge-reader logs exist for the Redwood City HQ and the server room housing the ALMA domain controller, but nobody reviews them on a cadence — they are pulled only when an investigation asks. Failed-access alerting is not configured. Physical monitoring is proportionate to a 300-person single-site company, yet the review loop is missing.
Q2 Examine: Badge system log retention settings, sample month of access logs
Q3 Observations: The August remediation landed: failed-access and after-hours alerts from the badge system now post to the D&R Slack channel, and the monthly fifteen-minute badge-log review is on the D&R checklist with two completed cycles. Execution is new enough that one review ran a week late, and alert thresholds are still generating occasional noise from the cleaning crew's after-hours entries — tuning is in progress.
Q3 Examine: Alert configuration; two monthly review records
Q3 Test: Badge failed-access test generated a Slack alert within one minute
Q4 Observations: Consistent execution reached: all three monthly badge-log reviews completed on schedule, after-hours alert noise was tuned out with a scheduled-access exception list, and the December review flagged and resolved one genuine anomaly — a terminated contractor badge that HR offboarding had missed, which also fed a fix into the Workday deprovisioning trigger. Proportionate physical monitoring for a single-site company, now with the loop closed.
Q4 Examine: Q4 review records; anomaly ticket and resolution
Action Plan: Configure failed-access and after-hours alerts into the D&R Slack channel; add a monthly 15-minute badge-log review to the D&R checklist.

Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: Personnel activity and technology usage are monitored to find potentially adverse events
Implementation Example: Ex2: Monitor logs from logical access control systems to find unusual access patterns and failed access attempts
Implementation Description: Personnel activity monitoring includes: (1) CloudTrail logging all IAM actions, (2) SSO authentication logs via Windows Authenticator and Palo Alto 2FA, (3) failed authentication alerting and tracking. The IAM team reviews anomalies daily. SOC dashboards (60% complete) will provide consolidated access pattern visibility.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review CloudTrail IAM logging
2. Examine SSO authentication logs
3. Verify failed authentication alerting
4. Assess SOC dashboard progress
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: Logical access monitoring covers AWS well via CloudTrail, but the on-prem ALMA Windows domain — including the 2012 fileserver — generates authentication logs that reach no monitored destination. Unusual-pattern detection for workforce accounts is not implemented; the INC-2024-02 SSH key abuse would still be visible only in AWS-side telemetry today.
Q2 Examine: CloudTrail IAM event coverage, domain controller audit policy
Q2 Test: Failed domain logon burst on a test account generated no alert
Q3 Observations: No score movement: ALMA domain authentication logs — including the 2012 fileserver — still reach no monitored destination, and workforce anomalous-pattern detection remains unimplemented, so the on-prem half of the INC-2024-02 pattern is still invisible. Log shipping design was completed alongside the PR.PS-04 central-storage work, but implementation queued behind the rota and encryption deliverables in the team's capacity plan.
Q3 Examine: Log shipping design doc; capacity plan showing sequencing
Q4 Observations: Held at 3 with a nuance worth recording: domain logs began shipping to central storage in December under the PR.PS-04 work, so generation and retention now exist — but no detections consume them, and impossible-travel and failed-logon analytics remain unbuilt, so monitoring value is still zero. The fileserver's December decommission shrinks the on-prem log surface. Detection build is committed in the FY2027 plan alongside the correlation use cases.
Q4 Examine: Central storage ingestion records; FY2027 detection plan
Action Plan: Build failed-logon and impossible-travel detections on the now-centralized domain logs by 2027-03-31; validate against the INC-2024-02 pattern replay (risk R3).

Function Description: Possible cybersecurity attacks and compromises are found and analyzed
Category Description:
Anomalies
indicators of compromise and other potentially adverse events are analyzed to characterize the events and detect cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: External service provider activities and services are monitored to find potentially adverse events
Implementation Example: Ex1: Monitor remote and onsite administration and maintenance activities that external providers perform on organizational systems
Implementation Description: External provider activity is not distinctly monitored: vendor and contractor sessions blend into normal telemetry, and the contractor-laptop origin of INC-2024-02 shows the cost. ServiceNow tracks which suppliers have access, but there is no linkage from that inventory to session-level monitoring or alerting on anomalous vendor behavior — a gap that compounds risk R6..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: External provider activity is not distinctly monitored: vendor and contractor sessions blend into normal telemetry, and the contractor-laptop origin of INC-2024-02 shows the cost. ServiceNow tracks which suppliers have access, but there is no linkage from that inventory to session-level monitoring or alerting on anomalous vendor behavior — a gap that compounds risk R6.
Q2 Examine: Supplier access inventory in ServiceNow, contractor access provisioning records
Q3 Observations: Vendor identity tagging started: contractor and supplier accounts in AWS and the ALMA domain are now labeled with their ServiceNow supplier record, covering roughly 70 percent of known external identities. Tagging alone changes nothing an attacker sees — no alerting consumes the tags yet — but it is the prerequisite that makes vendor-scoped monitoring possible, and it surfaced four orphaned contractor accounts that were disabled on discovery.
Q3 Examine: Identity tag coverage report; orphaned account tickets
Q4 Observations: First monitoring value delivered: out-of-hours and out-of-scope access alerts are live for the eight critical-tier suppliers, including both biometric-data processors, and fired twice in December — one confirmed benign maintenance window, one policy conversation with the vendor. Coverage below the critical tier remains tag-only, and TPRM criticality data does not yet auto-drive detection priority. Meaningful progress on risk R6, still short of consistent full-scope execution.
Q4 Examine: Alert configurations; two December alert dispositions
Q4 Test: Simulated after-hours vendor login generated the expected alert
Action Plan: Extend vendor-scoped alerting from the critical tier to all tiered suppliers and auto-join TPRM criticality to detection priority by 2027-06-30 (risk R6).

Function Description: The organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy expectations and policy are established communicated and monitored

documenting

Implementation Example: Ex1: Establish criteria for using a quantitative approach to cybersecurity risk analysis, and specify probability and exposure formulas
Implementation Description: Risk scoring today is a qualitative five-by-five matrix in ServiceNow with defined likelihood and impact anchors — documented and applied consistently, but there is no quantitative methodology: no probability formulas, no exposure-in-dollars calculation, no loss-exceedance view. For a company whose board asks what the 2024 incidents cost, the absence of dollar-denominated risk analysis is the largest single gap in risk governance..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Risk scoring today is a qualitative five-by-five matrix in ServiceNow with defined likelihood and impact anchors — documented and applied consistently, but there is no quantitative methodology: no probability formulas, no exposure-in-dollars calculation, no loss-exceedance view. For a company whose board asks what the 2024 incidents cost, the absence of dollar-denominated risk analysis is the largest single gap in risk governance.
Q2 Examine: Risk scoring procedure, ServiceNow scoring configuration
Q2 Test: Reviewed scoring consistency across 10 register entries
Q3 Observations: The FAIR-style quantification pilot ran in Q3 on the top three strategic risks (R3 detection, R5 trust, R6 supplier): loss magnitude and frequency ranges were estimated with calibrated inputs from Finance and Customer Success, producing first dollar-denominated exposure ranges. Method documentation exists but application is not yet consistent across the register — three of six strategic risks quantified, operational risks untouched.
Q3 Examine: Pilot methodology and three risk quantifications
Q4 Observations: Quantification extended to all six strategic risks in November, and the first loss-exceedance view was presented to the Audit Committee in December against the appetite line derived from insurance limits — the deliverable the action plan named. The committee used it to compare two FY2027 investment options. Consistent application at strategic tier is achieved; operational-tier quantification remains 2027 work.

Function Description: The organization's current cybersecurity risks are understood

assets
and individuals is understood by the organization
Subcategory Description: Cyber threat intelligence is received from information sharing forums and sources
Implementation Example: Ex1: Configure cybersecurity tools and technologies with detection or response capabilities to securely ingest cyber threat intelligence feeds
Implementation Description: Threat intelligence consumption is informal: D&R monitors vendor advisories, CISA alerts, and community sources, and GuardDuty's managed findings embed AWS threat intel — but there is no structured ingestion into detection tooling, no documented intelligence requirements tied to the threat profile, and no record of intel-driven detection or patching decisions. Intelligence use depends on individual analyst initiative..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Threat intelligence consumption is informal: D&R monitors vendor advisories, CISA alerts, and community sources, and GuardDuty's managed findings embed AWS threat intel — but there is no structured ingestion into detection tooling, no documented intelligence requirements tied to the threat profile, and no record of intel-driven detection or patching decisions. Intelligence use depends on individual analyst initiative.
Q2 Examine: D&R source list, GuardDuty configuration
Q3 Observations: Intelligence consumption formalized substantially: priority intelligence requirements were derived from the five-actor threat profile, the weekly intel triage launched inside the new ROC cadence with a decision log recording actions taken, and two triage-driven outcomes are already documented — an emergency patch pulled forward for an actively exploited CVE and a new GuardDuty suppression-rule review. Detection use-case development from intel remains unstarted, keeping this just short of consistent.
Q3 Examine: PIR document; triage decision log
Q4 Observations: The weekly triage ran without missed cycles through Q4 and the decision log shows steady action linkage, but the planned intel-to-detection pipeline did not materialize — no new detection use cases were built from intelligence this quarter as D&R capacity went to the DR test and tabletop. The process is consistent in its triage function while incomplete against its designed scope, warranting the hold at just-below-minimum until use-case development demonstrates.
Q4 Examine: Q4 triage logs; detection use-case backlog
Action Plan: Deliver the first two intel-derived detection use cases in Q1 2027 from the standing PIR set, with D&R capacity protected by the new hire.

Function Description: The organization's current cybersecurity risks are understood

assets
and individuals is understood by the organization
Subcategory Description: Potential impacts and likelihoods of threats exploiting vulnerabilities are identified and recorded
Implementation Example: Ex1: Business leaders and cybersecurity risk management practitioners work together to estimate the likelihood and impact of risk scenarios and record them in risk registers
Implementation Description: Likelihood and impact estimation happens jointly between security and business owners for the strategic register — each of R1 through R6 carries documented likelihood and impact rationale agreed with the accountable executive, reviewed monthly. The flaw is granularity and method: estimates are qualitative matrix placements without probability calibration, and operational risks below the strategic tier get scored by the GRC lead alone without business input..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Likelihood and impact estimation happens jointly between security and business owners for the strategic register — each of R1 through R6 carries documented likelihood and impact rationale agreed with the accountable executive, reviewed monthly. The flaw is granularity and method: estimates are qualitative matrix placements without probability calibration, and operational risks below the strategic tier get scored by the GRC lead alone without business input.
Q2 Examine: Risk register likelihood/impact fields, review minutes
Q3 Observations: The calibrated estimation pilot launched under the quantitative risk work: R2, R3, and R6 received calibrated probability and dollar-impact ranges built jointly with their accountable owners, replacing matrix placements, and the business-owner participation threshold for operational risks was defined and socialized. Three of six strategic risks remain on qualitative placements pending the Q4 pilot extension, and the estimation guide is one facilitator deep — process reliability now demonstrated but not yet resilient.
Q3 Examine: Calibrated estimates for R2/R3/R6; participation threshold memo
Q4 Observations: All six strategic risks completed calibrated estimation by December, and the loss-exceedance view built on the ranges anchored the FY2027 budget submission — the first investment case argued in dollars-at-risk terms. Business-owner participation held for all above-threshold operational estimates this quarter. The control now executes consistently with its metrics reviewed monthly; second-facilitator training is underway to remove the single-person dependency, the residual flaw.
Q4 Examine: Complete calibrated register; FY2027 budget submission excerpt
Q4 Test: Re-performed one calibration session's range arithmetic
Action Plan: Introduce calibrated estimation for the strategic register as part of the Q3 quantitative pilot under GV.RM-06, with business-owner participation required for operational risks above a defined threshold.

Implementation Description: Change management improved from the near-absent state the Q1 audit found — a documented procedure now exists, production changes route through pull-request review with the infrastructure-as-code gate, and an exceptions log started in April. Execution is unreliable: emergency changes bypass documentation roughly a quarter of the time, risk impact assessment is a checkbox rather than an analysis, and the exceptions log has no review cadence yet..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review change management policy
2. Examine ServiceNow change records
3. Sample audit change compliance
4. Verify crown jewel additional review process
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: Change management improved from the near-absent state the Q1 audit found — a documented procedure now exists, production changes route through pull-request review with the infrastructure-as-code gate, and an exceptions log started in April. Execution is unreliable: emergency changes bypass documentation roughly a quarter of the time, risk impact assessment is a checkbox rather than an analysis, and the exceptions log has no review cadence yet.
Q2 Examine: Change procedure, PR review records, exceptions log
Q2 Test: Sampled 10 Q2 production changes; 7 fully documented
Q3 Observations: Change management improvements held but did not deepen: the 48-hour emergency-change retrospective rule was adopted with compliance near 70% — better than the roughly-75%-documented baseline yet still leaking — the narrative risk-impact field deployed to the template, and the exceptions log gained its monthly CISO staff review in September's session. Execution remains unreliable under pressure; two August emergency changes bypassed documentation entirely during the ASM onboarding push.
Q3 Examine: Emergency change records; exceptions log; September review minutes
Q3 Test: Sampled 12 changes: 9 fully documented with risk narrative
Q4 Observations: The pattern repeated in Q4: routine changes flow through the documented pull-request path reliably, but the quarter's high-tempo work — encryption rollout, DR test remediation, host retirements — produced three more undocumented emergency changes, and retrospective compliance plateaued around 70%. The monthly exceptions review runs and surfaces the misses, which is new visibility, but the control's execution reliability has not structurally improved. Root cause is capacity and culture, not process design.
Q4 Examine: Q4 change sample; exceptions log trend
Q4 Test: Sampled 12 changes: 8 fully documented
Action Plan: Make emergency-change retrospective completion a squad-level KPI in the weekly ROC cycle and pre-stage change templates for planned high-tempo work, targeting 90%+ compliance by Q2 2027.

Implementation Example: Ex1: Assess the authenticity and cybersecurity of critical technology products and services prior to acquisition and use
Implementation Description: Software authenticity verification is strong where the pipeline enforces it — Sigstore/Cosign signing and verification gates cover internally built artifacts, and base image provenance is checked at build time. Acquisition-side assessment is thinner: third-party software enters through procurement security review for major purchases, but developer-adopted open source dependencies are scanned for vulnerabilities without authenticity or provenance evaluation, and hardware authenticity relies on approved-supplier sourcing alone..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Software authenticity verification is strong where the pipeline enforces it — Sigstore/Cosign signing and verification gates cover internally built artifacts, and base image provenance is checked at build time. Acquisition-side assessment is thinner: third-party software enters through procurement security review for major purchases, but developer-adopted open source dependencies are scanned for vulnerabilities without authenticity or provenance evaluation, and hardware authenticity relies on approved-supplier sourcing alone.
Q2 Examine: Pipeline verification gates, procurement review records
Q2 Test: Confirmed Cosign gate blocks unsigned artifacts in staging
Q3 Observations: Pipeline-enforced authenticity held steady — Sigstore/Cosign gates and base-image provenance checks operated without exception — while the acquisition-side gap persisted: the dependency provenance work was scoped and the top-tier dependency set defined (38 packages by blast radius), but implementation waits on the Q4 pipeline window. One incident of note: an ASM-surfaced service was found running an unsigned internal build predating the signing mandate, now remediated and added to the verification sweep.
Q3 Examine: Pipeline gate logs; top-tier dependency list; unsigned-build remediation
Q4 Observations: Dependency provenance checks landed in December for the top-tier set: signature and source verification now gate the 38 highest-blast-radius packages at build time, with two packages initially failing verification — one stale mirror, one upstream signing change — both resolved through documented paths. Combined with the existing artifact signing, acquisition-side verification now executes consistently for the defined scope; extending beyond the top tier and to hardware provenance remains the improvement path.
Q4 Examine: Provenance gate configuration; failure resolution records
Q4 Test: Re-ran verification for 5 top-tier packages
Action Plan: Add dependency provenance checks (signature and source verification) to the build pipeline for the top-tier dependency set.

Function Description: Safeguards to manage the organization's cybersecurity risks are used

Implementation Example: Ex1: Logically segment organization networks and cloud-based platforms according to trust boundaries and platform types (e.g., IT, IoT, OT, mobile, guests), and permit required communications only between segments
Implementation Description: AWS segmentation is real: production, staging, and corporate VPCs are separated with security groups permitting required flows only, and the staging-to-production lateral path exploited in INC-2024-02 was closed with reviewed rules. Q2 Observations: AWS segmentation is real: production, staging, and corporate VPCs are separated with security groups permitting required flows only, and the staging-to-production lateral path exploited in INC-2024-02 was closed with reviewed rules. Weaknesses remain at the edges — the on-prem ALMA network is flat, and Kubernetes namespace-level policies within EKS are default-permissive, so a compromised pod has broader east-west reach than the VPC design implies.

Q2 Test: Attempted staging-to-production connection on the old INC-2024-02 path; blocked

Q3 Test: Verified an unauthorized cross-namespace connection is denied in staging

One planned exception list is documented and dated. The residual step to target is measured policy-drift review on a defined cadence.

Q4 Test: Re-ran the cross-namespace denial test against production policies
Action Plan: Implement default-deny Kubernetes network policies for production namespaces; segment the on-prem network from cloud egress paths.

Category Description: The hardware, software (e.g., firmware, operating systems, applications), and services of physical and virtual platforms are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect their confidentiality, integrity, and availability
Subcategory Description: Configuration management practices are established and applied
Implementation Example: Ex1: Establish, test, deploy, and maintain hardened baselines that enforce the organization's cybersecurity policies and provide only essential capabilities (i.e., principle of least functionality)
Implementation Description: Configuration management includes: (1) hardened baselines for Amazon Linux 2 and Ubuntu documented and enforced via automation, (2) baseline compliance monitoring through AWS Config, (3) deviation alerting and remediation tracking. Legacy Windows 2012 fileserver upgrade is planned for Q3. 95% of systems deployed to hardened baselines.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review hardened baselines documentation
2. Verify AWS Config compliance
3. Examine deviation alerting
4. Assess legacy Windows 2012 upgrade plan
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: Hardened baselines exist and are applied for Amazon Linux 2 instances and EKS node groups, with drift checked at build time. Coverage is the weakness justifying the score: the legacy Ubuntu hosts run no enforced baseline, the Windows 2012 fileserver cannot meet the baseline at all (EOL), and workstation configuration relies on SentinelOne policy rather than a full hardening standard. Baseline exceptions are known but lack documented risk acceptance.
Q2 Examine: Baseline standard doc, build pipeline hardening checks, exception list
Q2 Test: Compared two sampled production instances to baseline; both compliant
Q3 Observations: Coverage gaps closed on schedule: hardened baselines extended to the Ubuntu hosts in August, the Windows 2012 fileserver received a formal, dated risk acceptance pending its Q4 decommission, and the workstation hardening standard published with SentinelOne policy mapped to it. Baseline drift is now measured — build-time checks plus a monthly compliance report — bringing the control to target with every known exception documented and dated.
Q3 Examine: Ubuntu baseline configs; risk acceptance record; workstation standard; drift report
Q3 Test: Sampled one Ubuntu host against the baseline checklist
Q4 Observations: Held at target and the last exception retired: the fileserver decommission in December removed the only asset that could not meet baseline, monthly drift reports ran clean with two deviations caught and corrected within SLA, and the workstation standard survived its first quarter of enforcement without material exceptions. Platform hardening is now uniform, measured, and free of standing exceptions for the first time in Alma's history.
Q4 Examine: Q4 drift reports; decommission record
Action Plan: Extend baselines to Ubuntu hosts; document formal risk acceptance for Windows 2012 pending decommission; publish a workstation hardening standard.

Function Description: Safeguards to manage the organization's cybersecurity risks are used
Category Description: The hardware, software (e.g., firmware, operating systems, applications), and services of physical and virtual platforms are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect their confidentiality, integrity, and availability
Subcategory Description:
Software is maintained
replaced
and removed commensurate with risk
Implementation Example: Ex1: Perform routine and emergency patching within the timeframes specified in the vulnerability management plan
Implementation Description: Patching SLAs are defined in the vulnerability management plan — 7 days critical, 30 days high — and cloud fleet patching meets SLA at roughly 90% via automated node recycling. Misses concentrate in the same legacy corners: Ubuntu hosts patch manually and slip SLA regularly, and the Windows 2012 fileserver receives no vendor patches at all, making it a standing SLA exception rather than a managed risk..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Patching SLAs are defined in the vulnerability management plan — 7 days critical, 30 days high — and cloud fleet patching meets SLA at roughly 90% via automated node recycling. Misses concentrate in the same legacy corners: Ubuntu hosts patch manually and slip SLA regularly, and the Windows 2012 fileserver receives no vendor patches at all, making it a standing SLA exception rather than a managed risk.
Q2 Examine: VM plan SLAs, Q2 patch compliance report, emergency patch records
Q2 Test: Sampled three critical CVEs from Q2; two patched within 7 days, one at 12 days on an Ubuntu host
Q3 Observations: Ubuntu patching automated in August using the same recycling pattern as the cloud fleet, ending the manual slippage — September SLA compliance reached 94 percent overall, with the Windows 2012 fileserver remaining the sole standing exception under its documented risk acceptance and Q4 terminal date. One consistent quarter at full scope minus one dated exception earns the passing score; the exception's removal is already scheduled.
Q3 Examine: Automated patching configs; September SLA report
Q3 Test: Verified a September critical patch reached Ubuntu hosts within the 7-day SLA
Q4 Observations: Reached target: the fileserver decommission eliminated the last SLA exception, December compliance held at 96 percent across the whole estate, and patch metrics — SLA compliance by severity, mean days-to-patch, exception count now zero — are reviewed monthly by the owner with drift acted on. Patching moved from a two-class system with a permanent asterisk to a single measured standard.
Q4 Examine: Q4 SLA reports; monthly metric review records
Action Plan: Automate Ubuntu patching or migrate hosts to Amazon Linux 2; hold the Windows 2012 decommission date as the terminal fix (PR.PS-03).

Function Description: Safeguards to manage the organization's cybersecurity risks are used

Subcategory Description:
replaced
and removed commensurate with risk
Hardware is maintained
Implementation Example: Ex1: Replace hardware when it lacks needed security capabilities or when it cannot support software with needed security capabilities
Implementation Description: Hardware lifecycle works by default in the cloud — nodes recycle continuously and laptops refresh on a three-year cycle. The score reflects the exception that defines the control: the domain-joined Windows 2012 fileserver is years past end of support, cannot support required security capabilities, and has survived two annual budget cycles as everyone's second priority.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Hardware lifecycle works by default in the cloud — nodes recycle continuously and laptops refresh on a three-year cycle. The score reflects the exception that defines the control: the domain-joined Windows 2012 fileserver is years past end of support, cannot support required security capabilities, and has survived two annual budget cycles as everyone's second priority. It is the single oldest risk in the environment.
Q2 Examine: Asset lifecycle policy, laptop refresh records, fileserver support status
Q3 Observations: The terminal fix is finally funded and moving: the fileserver migration to SharePoint Online was approved in July, content inventory and permission mapping completed in September covering all 1.1 terabytes of shares, and the decommission date is set for December. The score moves modestly — the EOL host still runs — but for the first time in three budget cycles the oldest risk in the environment has a funded, dated end.
Q3 Examine: Migration plan; content inventory; approved budget
Q4 Observations: Executed: content migrated to SharePoint Online through November, the Windows 2012 fileserver was powered down on December 5, its domain trust removed, and the hardware disposed with certificate of destruction. The single oldest risk in the environment is retired, taking with it the last shared service account, the last unpatchable host, and the last baseline exception. Hardware lifecycle management now has no known EOL assets in production.
Q4 Examine: Decommission ticket; domain trust removal record; destruction certificate
Q4 Test: Verified fileserver hostname no longer resolves and shares are unreachable
Action Plan: Fund and execute fileserver migration to SharePoint Online / managed storage in Q4; decommission the Windows 2012 host and remove its domain trust (feeds R4 asset hygiene).

Function Description: Safeguards to manage the organization's cybersecurity risks are used
Category Description: The hardware, software (e.g., firmware, operating systems, applications), and services of physical and virtual platforms are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect their confidentiality, integrity, and availability
Subcategory Description: Log records are generated and made available for continuous monitoring
Implementation Example: Ex1: Configure all operating systems, applications, and services (including cloud-based services) to generate log records
Implementation Description: Log generation is comprehensive on the cloud side — CloudTrail on all accounts, VPC Flow Logs, DNS query logs, ALB access logs, and EKS control-plane logging all feed the monitoring stack with defined retention. Gaps mirror DE.CM-03: on-prem Windows domain logs and legacy Ubuntu syslog reach no central destination, and application-level logs from two internal tools are generated but rotated locally into oblivion..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Log generation is comprehensive on the cloud side — CloudTrail on all accounts, VPC Flow Logs, DNS query logs, ALB access logs, and EKS control-plane logging all feed the monitoring stack with defined retention. Gaps mirror DE.CM-03: on-prem Windows domain logs and legacy Ubuntu syslog reach no central destination, and application-level logs from two internal tools are generated but rotated locally into oblivion.
Q2 Examine: Log source inventory, retention configuration, CloudTrail account coverage
Q2 Test: Confirmed CloudTrail enabled on all four AWS accounts including the sandbox
Q3 Observations: Held while the dependency built: cloud-side log generation stayed comprehensive with retention enforced, and the central-storage design for on-prem and legacy logs completed alongside the DE.CM-03 work — but ingestion had not started by fieldwork close, and the two internal application logs still rotate locally into oblivion. The score honestly stays put until logs actually land somewhere durable.
Q3 Examine: Central storage design; current ingestion inventory
Q4 Observations: Ingestion began: ALMA domain logs ship to central storage as of December with retention policy applied, and one of the two internal application logs was onboarded — the other retired with its host application's decommission. Generation and durable retention now cover effectively the full estate; the passing score reflects that completeness, while the gap to target is the detection consumption tracked under DE.CM-03 and the monitoring-value work it feeds.
Q4 Examine: Ingestion records; retention policy configuration
Q4 Test: Confirmed domain controller security events queryable in central storage
Action Plan: Onboard on-prem and legacy-host logs to central storage with retention policy applied (shared plan with DE.CM-03 remediation).

Function Description: Safeguards to manage the organization's cybersecurity risks are used
Category Description: The hardware, software (e.g., firmware, operating systems, applications), and services of physical and virtual platforms are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect their confidentiality, integrity, and availability
Subcategory Description: Installation and execution of unauthorized software are prevented
Implementation Example: Ex1: When risk warrants it, restrict software execution to permitted products only or deny the execution of prohibited and unauthorized software
Implementation Description: Unauthorized software prevention is implemented through: (1) SentinelOne application control on workstations and laptops, (2) app control policy defining permitted software, (3) Kubernetes node restrictions (shared SSH key remediation in progress). Server-side controls expand SentinelOne enforcement. Policy exceptions require CISO approval.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review SentinelOne application control
2. Verify app control policy
3. Examine Kubernetes node restrictions
4. Assess shared SSH key remediation
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: SentinelOne blocks known-malicious execution on managed endpoints and quarantine works — tested this quarter. But prevention of unauthorized software stops at malware: there is no allowlisting or restriction of unapproved-but-legitimate tools, developer laptops run unvetted utilities by design culture, and the fileserver will execute anything. For the contractor-laptop threat pattern that produced INC-2024-02, EDR-only is a partial answer.
Q2 Examine: SentinelOne policy export, software inventory sample from 10 endpoints
Q2 Test: EICAR execution blocked and quarantined on sampled laptop
Q3 Observations: The allowlisting pilot outperformed its plan: monitor mode on the twenty-five highest-privilege endpoints generated a clean approved-software catalog within six weeks, converted to enforce mode in September with a working exception path, and blocked two unvetted utilities without a single business-disruption ticket. Combined with SentinelOne's malware prevention, execution control on the endpoints that matter most is now measured, trending, and demonstrably improving — the direct answer to the contractor-laptop pattern of INC-2024-02.
Q3 Examine: Enforce-mode configs; catalog and exception records; block events
Q3 Test: Attempted execution of an uncataloged utility on a pilot endpoint was blocked
Q4 Observations: Sustained at fully optimized: enforce mode held across the privileged-endpoint tier with exception requests averaging under one day to disposition, block telemetry feeds the weekly D&R review, and the quarter's trend shows shrinking exception volume as the catalog matures. Deliberately not expanding to the general workstation fleet this year — the rubric's proportionality principle applies, and the marginal risk there does not justify the friction yet.
Q4 Examine: Q4 block and exception telemetry; trend review records
Action Plan: Pilot application allowlisting in monitor mode on the 25 highest-privilege endpoints; define an approved-software catalog with an exception path.

Function Description: Safeguards to manage the organization's cybersecurity risks are used
Category Description: The hardware, software (e.g., firmware, operating systems, applications), and services of physical and virtual platforms are managed consistent with the organization's risk strategy to protect their confidentiality, integrity, and availability
Subcategory Description:
Secure software development practices are integrated
and their performance is monitored throughout the software development life cycle
Implementation Example: Ex1: Protect all components of organization-developed software from tampering and unauthorized access
Implementation Description: The secure development pipeline is a genuine bright spot: all build artifacts are signed with Sigstore/Cosign and verified at deploy, source is protected with mandatory review and branch protection, dependency scanning gates merges, and the SDLC checklist embeds security review for changes touching authentication or biometric data paths. Signing adoption is measured (100% of production deploys verified this quarter) — the control is consistently executed and monitored..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: The secure development pipeline is a genuine bright spot: all build artifacts are signed with Sigstore/Cosign and verified at deploy, source is protected with mandatory review and branch protection, dependency scanning gates merges, and the SDLC checklist embeds security review for changes touching authentication or biometric data paths. Signing adoption is measured (100% of production deploys verified this quarter) — the control is consistently executed and monitored.
Q2 Examine: Cosign verification logs, branch protection settings, SDLC checklist for two sampled releases
Q2 Test: Attempted deploy of an unsigned test artifact to staging; rejected at admission control
Q3 Observations: The pipeline bright spot reached target formally: Sigstore/Cosign verification held at 100 percent of production deploys for a second consecutive quarter, dependency scanning gate-blocked two vulnerable upgrades before merge, and the SDLC checklist's authentication-path security review caught one design flaw pre-implementation. Signing adoption, gate effectiveness, and review coverage are all measured and reviewed — consistent execution with working metrics.
Q3 Examine: Deploy verification logs; gate block records; SDLC review records
Q3 Test: Attempted deploy of an unsigned test artifact was rejected
Q4 Observations: Improving beyond target: three consecutive quarters of 100 percent verified deploys establish a genuine trend, the team added provenance attestations to the build pipeline in November — deepening supply-chain integrity ahead of any customer requirement — and gate metrics show vulnerable-dependency attempts declining as engineering habits adapt. This control is the standing rebuttal to anyone who thinks a 300-person company cannot run a hardened pipeline.
Q4 Examine: Q4 verification logs; provenance attestation configs; gate trend data

Function Description: Assets and operations affected by a cybersecurity incident are restored
Category Description: Restoration activities are coordinated with internal and external parties
Subcategory Description: Recovery activities and progress in restoring operational capabilities are communicated to designated internal and external stakeholders
Implementation Example: Ex1: Securely share recovery information, including restoration progress, consistent with response plans and information sharing agreements
Implementation Description: Recovery-progress communication is handled through the same incident Slack channel and executive digest as response updates, with no distinct recovery-status cadence for internal stakeholders and none defined for customers awaiting service restoration. During INC-2024-01, customers learned restoration status from the public statement rather than direct channels — a pattern the rebuilt notification procedure addresses for breach disclosure but not for ongoing recovery updates..
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Recovery-progress communication is handled through the same incident Slack channel and executive digest as response updates, with no distinct recovery-status cadence for internal stakeholders and none defined for customers awaiting service restoration. During INC-2024-01, customers learned restoration status from the public statement rather than direct channels — a pattern the rebuilt notification procedure addresses for breach disclosure but not for ongoing recovery updates.
Q2 Examine: IR communications matrix recovery section; INC-2024-01 customer communications timeline
Q3 Observations: The communications matrix now defines a recovery-status cadence — daily internal digest during restoration and a drafted customer restoration-notice template — closing the gap INC-2024-01 exposed when customers learned status from the public statement. The cadence exists on paper and the internal leg ran once during a September service degradation, but the customer-facing leg has never been exercised and the template remains unapproved by counsel.
Q3 Examine: Communications matrix v2, draft customer restoration notice, September degradation Slack digest
Q4 Observations: The customer restoration-notice template was counsel-approved in November and the full recovery-communication cadence — internal daily digest plus customer-facing status per SLA — was exercised end-to-end in the December recovery tabletop, with Customer Success sending timed notices to a mock tenant list. Execution was clean but remains exercise-only; no live recovery event has yet used the cadence, which keeps this just below the passing floor.
Q4 Examine: Approved notice template, December tabletop communications log, cadence section of communications matrix
Q4 Test: Walkthrough of tabletop notice timing against SLA commitments
Action Plan: Apply the recovery-status cadence in the next live incident or Q1 2027 exercise and capture timing evidence; confirm Customer Success ownership of the notice send step.

Function Description: Assets and operations affected by a cybersecurity incident are restored
Category Description: Restoration activities are coordinated with internal and external parties
Subcategory Description: Public updates on incident recovery are shared using approved methods and messaging
Implementation Example: Ex1: Follow the organization's breach notification procedures for recovering from a data breach incident
Implementation Description: Public recovery messaging now follows the counsel-reviewed breach notification procedure with approved spokespeople and templates — a direct correction of the INC-2024-01 disclosure dispute that collapsed public trust (R5). The procedure is solid; the track record is one clean Q1 2026 execution, and the public trust score remains below the 90% target set for January 2027.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Public recovery messaging now follows the counsel-reviewed breach notification procedure with approved spokespeople and templates — a direct correction of the INC-2024-01 disclosure dispute that collapsed public trust (R5). The procedure is solid; the track record is one clean Q1 2026 execution, and the public trust score remains below the 90% target set for January 2027. Given that Alma sells authentication trust, this control carries a raised target: message discipline during recovery is a product-survival control, not a communications nicety.
Q2 Examine: Approved messaging templates; Q1 2026 public statement and approval chain
Q2 Test: Traced Q1 statement through the documented approval chain
Q3 Observations: The first quarterly message-approval drill ran in September with Marketing and counsel, rehearsing spokesperson assignments and template selection for a simulated recovery announcement. The breach-notification procedure remains solid and the drill surfaced one stale approver contact, since corrected. Public trust score reached the low 80s against the 90% January 2027 target (R5); the discipline is building but the track record is still one live execution plus one drill.
Q3 Examine: September drill record, spokesperson roster, trust-score trend report to the board
Q4 Observations: The second quarterly drill ran in December inside the recovery tabletop, pairing message approval with the customer restoration notices for the first time. Trust score continued climbing toward — but not to — the 90% target, tracked at each board cycle per the R5 treatment plan. Message discipline is now practiced on a cadence; the raised target reflects that recovery messaging is a product-survival control for an authentication company, and two drills plus one live execution do not yet evidence that bar.
Q4 Examine: December drill record inside tabletop after-action report, Q4 board trust-score slide
Q4 Test: Re-performance of approval chain timing during the tabletop
Action Plan: Continue quarterly drills; hold the raised target until the trust score crosses 90% (R5, January 2027) and a live recovery event executes the messaging cleanly.

Function Description: Assets and operations affected by a cybersecurity incident are restored
Category Description: Restoration activities are performed to ensure operational availability of systems and services affected by cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: The recovery portion of the incident response plan is executed once initiated from the incident response process
Implementation Example: Ex1: Begin recovery procedures during or after incident response processes
Implementation Description: The IR plan contains a recovery section with initiation steps, but recovery execution has never followed it end-to-end: INC-2024-01 recovery was improvised, and no incident since has exercised the full recovery path. AWS multi-AZ architecture gives strong platform resilience, which masks the process gap.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: The IR plan contains a recovery section with initiation steps, but recovery execution has never followed it end-to-end: INC-2024-01 recovery was improvised, and no incident since has exercised the full recovery path. AWS multi-AZ architecture gives strong platform resilience, which masks the process gap. The recovery procedures exist on paper and in architecture, not in practiced execution — the weakest link in Alma's incident lifecycle.
Q2 Examine: IR plan recovery section; INC-2024-01 postmortem recovery timeline
Q3 Observations: No change in demonstrated capability this quarter: recovery execution has still never followed the plan end-to-end. Q3 work was preparatory — the December full recovery exercise was scoped, restore targets selected (production PostgreSQL restore plus Windows domain recovery), and success criteria drafted as the acceptance gate for the recovery section. AWS multi-AZ resilience continues to mask the process gap; the score holds until execution evidence exists.
Q3 Examine: Exercise scoping document, recovery section success criteria draft
Q4 Observations: The full recovery exercise executed in December: restore-from-backup through service verification for the authentication API path succeeded within objectives, but the on-prem Windows domain recovery missed its recovery-time objective and two runbook steps proved stale. Gaps were folded back into the plan per the acceptance-gate commitment. Recovery execution now has one evidenced end-to-end run with known weaknesses — a real advance from paper-only, not yet consistent practice.
Q4 Examine: Exercise after-action report, updated recovery runbook, restore timing log
Q4 Test: Observed production-parity restore of PostgreSQL and domain controller rebuild
Action Plan: Re-run the Windows domain recovery leg in Q1 2027 after runbook corrections; set a second full exercise for H1 2027 to establish consistency.

Subcategory Description:
prioritized
Recovery actions are selected

and performed

Q2 Observations: There is no documented method for selecting and scoping recovery actions by priority — during INC-2024-01, restoration order was decided in the moment by the engineers present. The IR plan references 'available resources' but names no criteria linking recovery order to service criticality. Individual engineers know the platform well, so outcomes have been acceptable, but the capability is person-dependent, the R1 staffing risk applies directly.
Q2 Examine: IR plan recovery section; INC-2024-01 restoration sequence notes
Q3 Observations: Recovery-action selection remains person-dependent with no documented criteria; the quarter produced no direct change because the control is blocked on the business impact analysis under RC.RP-04, which drafted service-criticality tiers in September but has not yet published them.
