Function Description: Assets and operations affected by a cybersecurity incident are restored
Category Description: Restoration activities are performed to ensure operational availability of systems and services affected by cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: The integrity of backups and other restoration assets is verified before using them for restoration
Implementation Example: Ex1: Check restoration assets for indicators of compromise, file corruption, and other integrity issues before use
Implementation Description: Backup integrity verification includes: (1) automated checksums for S3 backups, (2) monthly integrity validation by DR Manager, (3) quarterly restore testing to verify recoverability. The S3 Bucket Security project enhances backup protection with versioning and cross-region replication. Restoration assets are checked for IOCs before recovery use.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Test Procedure(s):
1. Review backup checksum process
2. Verify monthly integrity validation
3. Examine quarterly restore testing
4. Assess S3 Bucket Security project
Q1 Observations: Per Internal Audit Q1 2026 assessment (IA-2026-001); see IA workpapers.
Q2 Observations: Backup integrity verification is defined for PostgreSQL — automated restore tests run monthly in staging with checksum validation — but the practice began in Q1 2026 and has two completed cycles, with one failure (a snapshot restored with stale WAL segments) still under investigation. Pre-restoration IOC checking is not yet part of the procedure. Consistent with the IA Q1 score of 2 moving to 3: the control is young and its scope is incomplete.
Q2 Examine: Monthly restore-test job output; backup verification procedure
Q2 Test: Reviewed May restore-test evidence including the failed WAL-segment case
Q3 Observations: Backup integrity verification reached consistent execution: the third and fourth monthly staging restore cycles completed clean, the stale-WAL-segment failure was root-caused to snapshot ordering and closed in October scope, and IOC plus corruption checks were added to the restore-test procedure ahead of the October due date. PostgreSQL coverage is now reliable with evidence for the period; extension to S3 configuration exports and the on-prem fileserver remains outstanding.
Q3 Examine: Restore-test logs for July–September cycles, WAL failure closure record, updated procedure with IOC step
Q3 Test: Re-performance of one staging restore with checksum and IOC validation
Q4 Observations: The control held at consistent execution through Q4 — monthly restore tests clean, IOC checks operating — but scope extension stalled: S3 configuration-export verification was implemented in November while the on-prem fileserver leg slipped to Q1 2027 behind the domain-recovery runbook corrections from the December exercise. Consistency is established; the score holds at the floor because measurement of restore coverage across all backup classes is still incomplete.
Q4 Examine: Q4 restore-test logs, S3 export verification record, slip note in ServiceNow remediation record
Action Plan: Add IOC and corruption checks to the restore-test procedure; close the WAL-segment failure; extend verification to S3 configuration exports and the on-prem fileserver.

Function Description: Assets and operations affected by a cybersecurity incident are restored
Category Description: Restoration activities are performed to ensure operational availability of systems and services affected by cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description: Critical mission functions and cybersecurity risk management are considered to establish post-incident operational norms
Implementation Example: Ex1: Use business impact and system categorization records (including service delivery objectives) to validate that essential services are restored in the appropriate order
Implementation Description: No business impact analysis or system categorization records exist to define restoration order — service criticality lives in engineers' heads. The continuous-authentication API is obviously first, but dependencies (PostgreSQL clusters, identity event pipeline, customer admin console) have no documented service delivery objectives.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: No business impact analysis or system categorization records exist to define restoration order — service criticality lives in engineers' heads. The continuous-authentication API is obviously first, but dependencies (PostgreSQL clusters, identity event pipeline, customer admin console) have no documented service delivery objectives. Post-incident operational norms were never formally established after either 2024 incident. This gap also blocks RC.RP-02 remediation.
Q2 Examine: Searched for BIA and system categorization records; reviewed architecture docs
Q3 Observations: The lightweight business impact analysis started in September with Finance: Dana Okafor's team and engineering drafted service-criticality tiers for the top-ten services, anchoring the continuous-authentication API, PostgreSQL clusters, and identity event pipeline at the head of the restoration order. Draft tiers exist but service delivery objectives are not yet set and nothing is published; restoration order therefore still lives in engineers' heads for any real event this quarter.
Q3 Examine: BIA working papers, draft criticality tiers
Q4 Observations: The BIA for the top-ten services completed in November with recovery-order service delivery objectives published as the restoration-priority reference, and the December exercise sequenced against it. Coverage stops at the top ten — on-prem services including the Windows domain and fileserver have no documented objectives, which the exercise's missed domain RTO made conspicuous. Post-incident operational norms remain undefined, so the control advances but stays clearly below the floor.
Q4 Examine: Published restoration-priority reference, BIA sign-off, exercise sequencing log
Action Plan: Extend the BIA to on-prem services in Q1 2027 and define post-incident operational norms; review objectives annually with Finance.

Function Description: Assets and operations affected by a cybersecurity incident are restored
Category Description: Restoration activities are performed to ensure operational availability of systems and services affected by cybersecurity incidents
Subcategory Description:
The integrity of restored assets is verified
systems and services are restored
and normal operating status is confirmed
Implementation Example: Ex1: Check restored assets for indicators of compromise and remediation of root causes of the incident before production use
Implementation Description: Restored-asset verification exists informally: after INC-2024-02 the rebuilt staging nodes were checked for the malicious SSH key and root-cause remediation was confirmed before reconnecting. But there is no documented verification checklist, no defined 'normal operating status' criteria, and no sign-off step.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Restored-asset verification exists informally: after INC-2024-02 the rebuilt staging nodes were checked for the malicious SSH key and root-cause remediation was confirmed before reconnecting. But there is no documented verification checklist, no defined 'normal operating status' criteria, and no sign-off step. Kubernetes deployments from signed images (Sigstore/Cosign) give implicit integrity for containers; hosts and data stores rely on individual diligence.
Q2 Examine: INC-2024-02 node rebuild notes; Cosign verification pipeline config
Q2 Test: Verified Cosign signature enforcement on one production deployment
Q3 Observations: A restored-asset verification checklist was drafted in August covering IOC scan, root-cause fix confirmation, service health criteria, and sign-off, formalizing the informal practice used after INC-2024-02. The checklist is in the recovery runbook as a draft; it has not been applied to any restoration, and defined normal-operating-status criteria exist only for the container tier where Sigstore/Cosign signing gives implicit integrity.
Q3 Examine: Draft verification checklist in recovery runbook v3
Q4 Observations: The verification checklist was adopted and exercised in the December recovery exercise: restored PostgreSQL and the rebuilt domain controller were checked against IOC, root-cause, and service-health criteria with recorded sign-off by Nadia Khan. Hosts and data stores now have a documented verification path rather than individual diligence. One exercised application is not yet consistent practice, and normal-operating-status criteria remain thin for on-prem assets.

Function Description: Actions regarding a detected cybersecurity incident are taken

Implementation Example: Ex1: Collect, preserve, and safeguard the integrity of all pertinent incident data and metadata (e.g., data source, date/time of collection) based on evidence preservation and chain-of-custody procedures
Implementation Description: Incident data collection relies on CloudTrail, GuardDuty findings, VPC Flow Logs, and SentinelOne telemetry, all of which are retained centrally in AWS. However, there is no documented evidence-preservation or chain-of-custody procedure: no forensic image was taken of the compromised staging node in INC-2024-02, and collection timestamps are not systematically recorded.
Auditor: Leila Haddad (Security GRC Lead) - CISO management self-assessment
Q2 Observations: Incident data collection relies on CloudTrail, GuardDuty findings, VPC Flow Logs, and SentinelOne telemetry, all of which are retained centrally in AWS. However, there is no documented evidence-preservation or chain-of-custody procedure: no forensic image was taken of the compromised staging node in INC-2024-02, and collection timestamps are not systematically recorded. For a company whose product is authentication trust, forensic defensibility is a material gap.
Q2 Examine: Log retention configuration; INC-2024-02 evidence inventory
Q3 Observations: The evidence-preservation SOP — snapshot, hash, custody log — was drafted under the IR Enhancement project this quarter but is not yet adopted; no incident this period required forensic collection, so practice is untested. Central retention of CloudTrail, GuardDuty, VPC Flow Logs, and SentinelOne telemetry continues, but the INC-2024-02 gap stands: there is still no operational procedure that would produce a defensible forensic image of a suspect host.
Q3 Examine: Draft evidence-preservation SOP
Q4 Observations: The SOP was adopted in November and EBS snapshot automation for suspect hosts pre-staged and fired successfully in the December tabletop, producing a hashed snapshot with a custody log entry inside 20 minutes of the simulated compromise call. Forensic defensibility now has a documented, once-exercised path for AWS assets; on-prem hosts remain manual, and no live incident has yet tested the chain end-to-end.
Q4 Examine: Adopted SOP, tabletop snapshot artifacts with hashes and custody log
Q4 Test: Observed snapshot automation run against a tabletop suspect node
Action Plan: Extend the SOP to on-prem hosts (domain controller, fileserver) and prove the chain on a live event; review custody log format with counsel in Q1 2027.
